items: Sketch your entire home floor plan and mark every narrow hallway, single-exit room, and enclosed space where you've been attacked more than once, marking your bathroom in red as a known attack location.
Rate each room for ambush potential by checking for hiding spots like furniture near walking paths, dark corners, and spaces behind doors where your cat can wait undetected for a pass-through.
Identify every room where obstacles block your path to the exit or that has only one door—these low-escape rooms are where attacks become most dangerous and require extra caution.
Map all window sightlines where your cat can watch outside and note which rooms face the street, other cats, or prey animals, since these locations build arousal that often redirects onto you.

Install a sliding door bolt at the top of your safe room door that you can operate with one hand while holding something, ensuring it locks from both sides so you can secure yourself inside or contain your cat.

Seal the gap under your safe room door with adhesive rubber weather stripping since most interior doors have 1-2 inch gaps where a determined cat can reach paws to scratch, bite, or wedge the door open.

Stock your safe room with a supply kit including bandages, antiseptic, your phone, water, a portable charger, and any medications your cat needs, allowing you to manage both of you for hours if necessary.
Test your safe room by locking yourself inside and attempting to exit from both sides while holding an object in one hand, verifying you can operate the lock quickly and escape without injury.
Close blinds or curtains at windows where your cat frequently watches outdoor triggers like birds, other cats, or movement, preventing arousal from building with no outlet.
Announce yourself with noise or a treat toss before entering narrow hallways or high-risk zones so your cat knows you're coming and isn't startled into an ambush response.
Keep a pillow or thick blanket between yourself and your cat when passing through ambush-prone areas, using it as a physical barrier that absorbs scratches or bites.
Feed, play, and interact with your cat at consistent times each day to create predictability that lowers overall stress and reduces the frequency of attack episodes.
